title: A Big Decision About a White House Ballroom
summary: A group called the National Capital Planning Commission said yes to President Trump's plan to build a ballroom at the White House. This group makes sure buildings in Washington, D.C., are okay to build. They think the ballroom is needed and will look nice. But a judge said they must wait because Congress needs to agree too. In other news, the Army's top leader, General Randy George, is leaving his job right away because the Defense Secretary asked him to. Also, Democratic leaders are going to court to stop President Trump from changing rules about mail-in voting. They say he doesn't have the power to do that. In Colorado, a court said Tina Peters, who lied about elections, should get a new sentence because her free speech was hurt.

a group of people who make decisions
a person in court who decides if something is fair
the part of the U.S. government that makes laws
the part of the military that fights on land
voting by sending your ballot in the mail

options: A ballroom at the White House
A new park in Washington, D.C.
questions.correct_answer: A ballroom at the White House

Why are Democratic leaders suing President Trump?
options: To stop changes to mail-in voting
questions.correct_answer: To stop changes to mail-in voting

questions.question: What did the judge say about the ballroom project?
What is Tina Peters known for?
Who will replace General George for now?

background_read: The White House is where the U.S. President lives and works. Washington, D.C., is the capital city of the United States. The National Capital Planning Commission helps decide what buildings can be built there. The Army is part of the military that protects the country. Mail-in voting lets people vote from home by sending their ballot in the mail. Courts help solve disagreements about laws and rules.
Article_Structure: WHO: The National Capital Planning Commission, President Trump, General Randy George, Democratic leaders, Tina Peters
WHAT: Approval of a White House ballroom, Army leader stepping down, lawsuit over mail-in voting, resentencing for election denier
WHERE: Washington, D.C., the White House, Colorado
WHY: To build a ballroom, due to leadership changes, to challenge voting rules, to address free speech in sentencing

perspectives.description: Some people think the ballroom is needed and will look good.
Others say Congress should approve it first.
Democratic leaders believe President Trump cannot change mail-in voting rules alone.
